Issue 	No.24 • July 2013
Why The ''Talk Radio'' E01ail Format
Blows All Other Methods
Out Of the Water
Hey Email Player...
Check it out... this month marks 2 years of "Email Players". Seems like I just
launched this bad-boy yesterday and here we are, 24 months later, and we haven't even
scratched the surface of all the super profitable ways you can use in your email marketing.
Since this is a milestone issue, I want to talk about something that has made my emails
stand out from all the other emails out there ... as well as y'all who have been following my
methodology. Listen, over the past several months there has been a lot of chatter about
writing emails in a soap opera "episodic" format where you leave people on cliflhangers
so they'll come back again next time. There's certainly nothing wrong with that approach.
And it can (and obviously does for some people) work. But, what I'm going to show you is
a way you can not only easily incorporate the "soap opera" way (if'n that's your bag), but
also give you the freedom to work in several other kinds of ways of selling. What I'm
talking about is the "talk radio" format. I remember Matt Furey teaching this back in the
day, and it works like gangbusters. In fact, I like to say email is nothing more than "talk
radio on glass." (i.e. the computer screen). Anyway, here's what I mean by the talk radio
format and why it works so well:

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
In America everyone knows what talk radio is. But if you're in a country where
maybe there is no talk radio, they are basically shows (usually lasting 3 hours for the big
syndicated ones) where a personality (or more than one personality) sits down and talks
about politics or whatever the format of the show is. So think Rush Limbaugh or Howard
Stern. These are the biggest and most influential (the former does politics, the latter
entertainment). I don't listen to Stern much, but used to listen to Rush a lot back in the
day (these days I listen to Michael Savage when I'm working out-- otherwise I don't have
much time for it). Anyway, so a few years ago one of the big magazines (forget which)
interviewed Rush about his methods. And he said he fills his head with the news of the
day by consuming all the big newspapers, watching all the various news programs, etc.
Then, comes in and just... talks. Stream of consciousness. No script or really any idea
what he's going to say during his opening monologue. That's email! Yes, you can have
notes and ideas (I do). But most of the fun of it is just talking (writing) about what's on
your mind. It could be a story about something that happened to you or someone you
know (like someone in the market). It could be a list of stuff you know your audience is
doing wrong you want to warn them about. It could be a Q&A as you remember a
comment someone sent you and you want to answer it. It could be a complaint you have
about a product or type of person you have to deal with in your market. It could be
anything. What's on your mind? What's bothering you? What arc you pleased about?
What are you scared of, happy about, or worried about? What about your audience?
What are they happy, freaked or worried about? What pains and problems docs your
audience have? What kind of prospect or customer would you like to repel off your list?
Have you seen any cool movies recently that reminded you about something regarding

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
your product, service or market? Hear any interesting analogies? See anything on TV that
can be related to your product or market? All these things are fair game to talk about --
whether it's in the form of a dramatic story or just you ranting off the cuff or giving your
opinion. This gives you a tremendous amount of freedom. It lets your mind roam,
connect ideas together -- which is what "creativity" really is -- and express whatever is on
your mind in a unique, fun and interesting way (and you should always tailor it to your
market) and then tic it into something you sell. (I showed 47 different ways to "segue" into
your call to action/pitch in the January 2013 issue-- see enclosed catalog for details).
Here are some of the benefits of using the talk radio format:
• 	Easy to write (Again -you're not boxed in to writing stories every day, most
people have a hard enough time writing as it is, what you want is to let your mind
be free to do whatever it wants, not have to fit everything into a specific structure)
• 	Direct response friendly. (Soap operas and TV are generally full of non-direct
response ads, yet radio is practically AIL direct response, there's a reason for
that... )
• 	Easily recyclable. (Write enough emails and you can start doing "encore
presentations" where you simply re-use emails you wrote 6, 9, 12 months earlier
with hardly anyone noticing. and even when they do, it's all good, and can often
nab you more sales. In fact, my favorite talk radio host Michael Savage does these
a lot -- and they're just as good and entertaining the second time as they were the
first)
• 	Sets your unique personality up as the leader. (Not just an "expert"-- but
someone people want to read, follow and, yes, buy from if you recommend a
solution to their problems or a way to fulfill their desires. Talk radio lets people get
to know, like and trust you. It's why these talk radio hosts get calls from people
saying, "you're like my best friend, I tune in every day. .. " and you get this reaction
from email subscribers if you do it right, too)
• 	Downright fun. (One of the things I wrote about in the "Email Players
Playbook" is email done right is not only therapeutic... but FUN. Once you get
into a groove of doing these, they are fun to write. If you have a pile of work to do
each day, writing your email first -- which I recommend doing, by the way -- is like
eating your dessert first. It's fun, and gets your mind working)

Want examples?
Just look at any of my emails. The ones I post on my blog or have reprinted in this
newsletter each month. They're just mini talk shows on glass (i.e. the computer screen -- I
know, screens aren't glass, but I think it sounds cool, so whatever...) Many of mine still
incorporate "open loops" (where I purposely leave the answer to a question or solution to
a problem unexplained -- except instead of closing the loop in another email, the loop is
closed on my sales page or in my product) and tell stories, etc. Anyway, bottom line? The
talk radio format has been the most profitable email format I know of. It's easy to write.
It's fun. Sets you up as a trusted LEADER (not just another "expert"). Is recyclable and
very direct response marketing friendly. Use it, enjoy it and, yes profit from it. ..
''The Man With The Hathaway Stein'':
David Ogilvy's Bizarre
E~nail List Building Secret
Back in the 1950's the great ad man David Ogilvy who (for all I know) may have
never even sent an email in his entire life... created a great (if not bizarre) way to help
build your email list, make you stand out in an overcrowded marketplace and, yes,
increase your sales. And he did it without using the Internet or technology of any kind.
Here's what happened: He'd nabbed the Hathaway account and was to create advertising
for their Hathaway shirts. Originally, David did the standard (although still brilliant) thing
of putting a Hathaway shirt on on a worldly-looking dude in an idealistic or rugged
background. The same sort of psychology that worked for Marlboro and, today, the Dos
Equis Man. The idea was to create a sense of ruggedness and badassery. Again, nothing
really "new" about that at the time. So what the genius Mr. Ogilvy did to spice things up
a bit was put a cheap little eyepatch on the model -- giving him a sort of swashbuckling,
daring and adventurous look. Not just a rugged sophisticated m.an ... but an
eccentric m.an. A man who smacked of excitement and danger ("why does he wear that
eye patch ... ?") Again, the 1950's version of the Dos Equis Man. Very cool idea. And, a
very profitable idea, too. The ads became both popular AND profitable... running in all
the major publications of the day and exploding sales.
Anyway, why am I telling you this story (most people with any sense of advertising

history have already heard)? How does this help your EMAIL list building? And, what's
the friggin' point???
Hey! Have some patience!
All will be explained in due course. But first, another advertising mini-lesson:
What you also may (or may not) already know is, peoples brains are "wired" to
remember unusual and bizarre and whacky things we see and hear. .. and completely
ignore and forget the mundane and boring stuff (i.e. 99% of all advertising and marketing
you see online, offline or on the sidelines). This is one reason I recommend tossing in a
"bizarre" email every now and then as talked about in the "Email Players Playbook". Or
to tell interesting stories and use weird, off-the-wall analogies and metaphors. Cuz those
kinds of emails are harder to ignore and forget.
Okay, now, "fast forward" to the present:
A few months ago I went to a German microbrewery injacksonville, Oregon. I
was there with a chick and we were having a merry old time tasting different beers.
Eventually, I found one I especially liked called "DOPPELBOCK" --an especially strong
and dark German beer. So I ordered a "stein" of it (a "stein" is a giant mug, either a half
liter or a full liter) and this beer had lots of alcohol in it. As I drank this monstrosity, I
noticed the stein was almost as big as my head and, in my buzzed stupor, I asked the girl I
was with to take a picture of me drinking it. I thought it'd be funny to send to my dad, if
nothing else. The result? A rather... bizarre ... dare I say "eccentric" photo of
Dle coDlpletely unlike any of the other photos I have. Especially unlike the more
professional photos I had on my website until then. Anyway, long story short, I got back
and realized ... this was my "Hathaway shirt" pic! This was the one photo of me that
completely stands out, completely conforms to my already anti-professional philosophy
(I've written about being an anti-professional on my blog if you want to know more about
what that means, and if you should adopt the same mentality), and would make a very
different (and cool, in my humble, but arrogant, opinion) photo for my splash squeeze
page. So I shot an email to my droogjim Yaghi (who helps me with these things) with the
photo and asked him to switch it out. A few hours later he had it done and the new splash
squeeze page was up and running. And I gotta say, the results have been awesome. My opt
ins started going up from that page... I was getting more comments and questions about it
(meaning it was sticking in peoples' minds) and it even spawned a few losers trying to

mimic it. Luckily those didn't last last long. In fact, that same day I changed my FaceBook
photo to that pic. And, predictably, a goo-roo fanboy changed his photo to a pic of him
drinking a beer (in a boring cup) and with a dopy smile and beer mustache. Of course, he
missed the point and looked like both a dork and a copy cat. I only bring this up so
anyone reading this understands it wasn't the stein and the beer and the look in my eye in
that photo that did the "selling." It was the originali~. sense of adventure and quirkiness
that did it. But mostly it was the originality. So in case a goo-roo fanboy has slipped past
my defenses and is reading this: Do not copy what I did. Do your OWN thing.
And that brings me to my point: If you want to increase your opt ins, sales,
personal branding power and sense of uniqueness amongst a sea of lazy, non-thinking
competitors... find your unique "Hathaway shirt" look. Again, be original. Don't slap
an eye patch on or drink out of a stein or do anything rese~nbling anything
else you've seen. You want to stand out in peoples' psychology, be seen as a leader not
a wannabe. A trendsetter not a follower. And to do that you must find your own unique
eccentric look. How, you ask? Well, you can (and should) think on it some. Let your mind
wander. Take in all the stimuli you see on TV in the movies, and in the culture (as well as
historical). You might get lucky (like I did-- I did not plan my stein picture out, it just. ..
happened). But if I had to do it over, realizing what an impact finding your own unique
Hathaway look can have, I'd go looking for a good photographer. I'd contact as many as
possible, and look at all their portfolios. (I actually did do this back in 2011 when I had
professional shots taken.) And I'd find the best one who fits my personality and what I'm
going for. And then, I'd TELL them exactly what my plan is. That I want that Hathaway
man look that's unique. Trust me, a good photographer can do this. He/ she will also give
you ideas (and have seen styles) that will make this task a lot easier. (You could also do this
with having a "cartoon" made of your likeness, too, btw, like jon McCulloch, Shameless
Shamus Brown, Matt Furey, etc have.) Anyway, I think everyone should have their own
hathaway shirt pic. Something that uniquely (key word UNIQUELY, don't be a loser
copy cat) sticks out, that will lay entrenched in peoples' psychology. Then, put that pic on
your splash squeeze page (you do have one of those, right? If not, check out thejuly 2012
Email Players issue -- see enclosed catalog for details), and on your social media pages,
and on your site. Do this and I really believe you'll stick out like a glowing thumb, attract
more people to you, and have a more profitable business as a result.
Plus, I am fairly certain David Ogilvy would approve, too...

Why You Know You're Doing It Right
When The Snowflakes Con1plain

Every now and then I get a complaint email from some snowflake who is either
super uptight, ridiculously politically correct or simply can't read anything in context. Or,
someone who is all of the above. I almost always ignore these complaints (they are not my
market). But, I also realize more often than not, I get more sales from the emails these
precious, easily-offended "snowflakes" complain about. So, when that happens, you are
almost always doing something RIGHT. Below is (1) the email being complained about
(2) the little snowflake's complaint and (3) an example of why YOU must write more
emails like this if you want to make more sales and stick out in a sea of other emails in the
inbox. Anyway, here's the "offending" email he read out of context:

Okay, so that's the email that was complained about. Now, here's the snowflake's
complaint:
"I feel you made a poor choice in today's em.ail. Your
use of 'slipping someone a m.ickey' as a metaphor is
fine on the face of it. Mickey Finn robbed people
after giving them. knockout drops. Had you used that
as your example, things would have been fine.
Instead, you used it as a metaphor for date rape,
which is something that's not even remotely funny
and seems uncouth, even for you. It happens, sure.
But I don't thjnk you needed to go there. Anyway, I'm.
not particularly upset. I'm not unsubscribing and
I'm not intending to rant. I just wanna say that I
think you could have achieved the same effect
without stepping on a land mine."
I must say, the delicate little snowflakes on the Internet are nothing if not
predictable. Guys like this (and they are likely on your list, too, so take note ...) are always
looking for reasons to be offended, and will eagerly read things completely out of context
(like he did) in order to put themselves on some kind of invisible pedestal. Ironically, his
was the only complaint. And just for kicks, I asked a few women I know what they
thought and they totally understood the metaphor (as has anyone who's been financially
raped by some black hat marketer bilking them out of their life savings putting them on
the street). And herein is the lesson. It's a very important lesson, too. And, one you will
have to make your peace with if you want your emails to have maximum impact,
maximum readership and, yes, have maximum sales. And that lesson is: The best
em.ails stir up emotions. They make crystal clear analogies that "say" far more than
can be said by trying to coldly explain facts or water them down with the least interesting
angles for fear of offending someone who will read them out of context. The best

example of this I can think of is in... wait for it. .. The Holy Bible. Specifically, the Book of
Judges. Towards the end of the book, the Israelites are in a state of apathy and moral
anarchy. A lot of bad people are getting away with a lot of bad stuff. And theft, rape and
even murder are way too common with nobody really doing anything about it. Anyway,
what happens is, a wandering Levite (the "priest" tribe of ancient Israel) is traveling with
his concubine, and one night she is viciously raped to death by some psychopaths who
lived amongst the Benjamites. The Levite (understandably) wanted justice. But due to the
times, he knew he had to do something radical to get anyone to care. So what did he do?
Beg for help? Start a posse? Give up? Uhm ... no. What this dude did was cut his
concubine's corpse into 12 pieces, and sent a piece to each of the 12 tribes!
Now THAT'S impact. There was no hemming and hawing. No "brushing it off." And no
ignoring the message. And you know what? Great email marketing also has this kind of
hardcore impact, too - and asks the exact same question the Levite no doubt did:
"What's it gonna take
to get peoples'
UNDMDED attention?"
Obviously, don't do anything gross. Remember, let's keep this in context, yeah? But
the mere act of asking the above question will generate all kinds of profitable ideas for
your email that make you instantly stick out. Those ideas can take the form of stories,
metaphors, analogies, or just using different kinds of words or language choices to get
your point across. All email marketing is, at the end of the day, is communication. A way
to get your message to your market. Yes, the little mush cookie snowflakes on your list will
object and complain. They will not "like" it. And, they will even offer suggestions on how
could have done it instead in a more watered down, wimpy, less controversial and less
IMPACTFUL way. I mean, if it was up to them, they'd rewrite judges, too, I bet. You
know, maybe the Levite should have wrote a nice note to the other tribes asking for help
saying pwease? Look, I see a lot of y'all's emails due to the critique you get each month.
And one thing I often notice is in many (not all) cases, you could be pushing harder. One
of my favorite personal branding authors Peter Montoya once did an interview with Dan
Kennedy. And he told Dan most people think they are going too far but aren't. In fact, he
said when you think you have gone too far don't worry, your market will let you know. I
have found that to be the case, too. Chances are you aren't pushing hard enough in your
emails. If you find that's the case, then push harder and see what happens. After all, email
is a very forgiving medium. And it's always better to ask forgiveness than rather than for
permission ...

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
How To Do Special Email Sales
Without Angering Previous Buyers
On Your List Who Paid Full Price
Back in the February 2013 "Email Playersn issue (see the enclosed catalog) I
showed you how to do special sales (and get fast, and potentially big) cash flow payouts.
And since then, a few subscribers have asked the one question I remember asking myself
when doing special sales: "If you haven't segmented buyers of that product off so you can
exclude them, how do you do these sales so you don't make them angry since they paid
full price?" And the answer is ... you don't have to "do" anything. What I'm about to say
spans multiple markets I've sold in, and different products and offers (ranging from low
ticket to super high ticket), with discounts going as deeply as 90%. And you know what
my experience has been? I have never once had anyone get "mad" (disappointed they
missed out, maybe, but not mad) at me for doing a sale because they paid full price. And
the reason why is because people buy when they want to buy. Not everyone needs a
discount to pull the buying trigger (and if they do, do you really want them on your list
anyway?) If you think about it, people who only wait for a sale or get mad
when something goes on sale after they bought are probably not going to be your best,
most pleasant customers anyway. I cannot even imagine getting mad, for example, at
Gary Bencivenga if he were to offer his $5k course at a discount. What do I care? I got a
TON of value from it, and I'd have paid double for it. Or, if you are selling to non-
business-to-business people (who are presumably more likely to be sensitive to price) try
thinking of it this way: Each year on "black Friday" (day after Thanksgiving in the United
States) everyone knows the big screen TV's are going to go on sale at Walmart or
whatever. Yet, people still buy those TV's a month, a week maybe even a day earlier
(maybe not a day. .. but ...) because they WANT that TV now and don't want to wait. They
don't later storm into the store yelling at the manager on duty, they don't even care.
(Chances are they're too busy watching Tv. ..)
Now, if you are still worried about it, you can simply give your previous buyers a
gift before the sale. Specifically, a gift that has more perceived value than the savings
everyone is getting. And there ya go. Problem solved. Don't let something like this stop
you from doing special sales with emails. It's one of the fastest and most reliable ways to
bring in cash flow you can use.
